name: Starfaring
description:
Starfaring takes place in a late, worn-out era of space travel. Two hundred years ago the jump drive made the colonization of hundreds of worlds possible. The golden age of the pioneers is long over. Today hyperspace is surveyed, charted, divided into leaseholds between the great Line conglomerates that control routes, fuel and tariffs. To survive as an independent captain of a jump ship is to balance constantly between cargo contracts, dwindling fuel, Line bureaucracy, and the quiet routes that nobody enters on the official charts.

The world does not feel vast and empty — it feels dense and watched. Drift stations hang like buoys in the shadow of gas giants, their corridors sticky with recycled air. Hyperspace corridors are as densely flown as old trade roads, and just as contested. There is the trade of smugglers, wreckers, freighters without papers; and there are the Lines, which know, tolerate, play off, or liquidate all three depending on the tone of voice.

Over everything floats the unspoken knowledge that jump physics is not as controllable as the Lines pretend. There are sectors where jumps take longer than the trip itself. There are ships that returned with every mirror reversed. There are radio signals pulsing in dead languages. The academies call these 'anomaly fields' and downplay them. On the drift stations they are called 'maws' — and captains who entered them either come back changed or do not come back at all.

There is no magic. No religion in the formal sense. But there is that old, cold respect for what humans have not measured. Crew contracts carry clauses against entering uncharted sectors. Cargo holds have shrines. Experienced navigators wear lucky charms they officially do not own.

Politically, the Line consortia are the real power. Local governments are puppets or petitioners. So-called 'Free Worlds' try to break away and are economically strangled for it. Consuls — the mid-level Line functionaries — are the faces of power, some pragmatic, some fanatic, all dangerous. A captain who does not operate in the shadow of these consuls pays double for every jump: in tariffs, in permits, in nerves.

In this world every journey begins with a simple truth: you have too little fuel for the official route, too much debt for the safe one — and always too many secrets for the quiet one.
player_role: Captain of an independent jump ship
starting_situation: You sit on the bridge of your jump ship — tanks half empty, cargo manifest awkward, the radio band thick with voices you shouldn't be hearing. A drift station hangs ahead in the shadow of a gas giant. Out there waits a contract, a Line inspection, and an anomaly your charts don't know.
narration_style: Cinematic, soberly technical with moments of wonder; short sentences under tension, calmer in shipboard routine. Address the captain as 'you'. Concrete sensory detail before explanation.
voice_sample: The reactors hum half a tone deeper than yesterday. On the radio: a woman's voice you don't know, calling you by name — quietly, almost kindly, in a language that should not exist.
mood: Lonely, tense, shot through with cold awe of the unknown — but never hopeless.
ambience: Humming reactors, ozone and cold cable dust, the ticking of cooling hull plates, sterile emergency lighting, the occasional spark in the void.
magic_physics: No magic. Hyperspace jumps need helium-3 and exact navigation windows; misjumps age matter or swallow ships. There are anomalous sectors where jump physics fails — no one knows why, and the Lines play it down.

regions.name: 'Wanderfalcon'
regions.description: An aging independent jump ship that serves as home, livelihood, and last refuge for its crew. The Bridge of the 'Wanderfalcon', the Maintenance Deck, and Cargo Bay 4-South are all cramped compartments inside a vessel held together by skill, debt, and stubbornness.

regions.name: Drift Station Kells
regions.description: A run-down drift station at the edge of charted space, dense with recycled air, gray commerce, and quiet surveillance. Drift Bar 'Last Jump', Warehouse 'Late Delivery', and Line Office Sector 12 all sit within its corridors, making Kells a perfect meeting point for smugglers, wreckers, captains, and consuls.

regions.name: Anomaly Corridor Vex
regions.description: A warped hyperspace corridor between sectors where jump timings slip, mirrors come back wrong, and charts feel like suggestions. Officially it is a survey problem; unofficially it is a maw that changes ships and the people inside them.

regions.name: Wreck Field Khorr-7
regions.description: A vast salvage graveyard of pioneer-era colonial ships drifting around a dead star. Wrecker clans prowl its slow debris tides for relics, sealed holds, and forgotten systems that still whisper on old power.

regions.name: Ice Moon Loke
regions.description: A frozen fuel frontier centered on an almost abandoned refueling station where helium-3 is cheap and survival is not. Blizzards, failing infrastructure, and desperate crews make every landing on Loke a calculation of cost versus frostbite.

regions.name: Free Worlds
regions.description: A loose patchwork of breakaway worlds trying to evade the tariff grip and route monopolies of the Lines. Their ports trade in defiance, necessity, and political theater, while figures like Consul Adira Mira fight strangulation with diplomacy and nerve.

places.name: Bridge of the 'Wanderfalcon'
places.description: Command center of your jump ship. Hyperspace console, worn star map, smell of old coffee and ozone.

places.name: Drift Station Kells
places.description: Run-down trading and smuggler station at the edge of charted space. Sticky corridors, recycled air, every second door a dubious opportunity.

places.name: Maintenance Deck
places.description: Lower deck of the ship. Yellow emergency light, clanking tools, oily air. Here you improvise everything the academy never taught.

places.name: Cargo Bay 4-South
places.description: Half-empty hangar in the belly of the 'Wanderfalcon'. A crate with a Line seal stands there that no one ordered.

places.name: Drift Bar 'Last Jump'
places.description: Station tavern on Kells. Meeting point of the wreckers, bar built from hyperspace debris, in the back room cards change owners.

places.name: Line Office Sector 12
places.description: Bureaucratic zone of the consuls. Marble lobby in a steel bubble. Politeness here costs processing fees.

places.name: Anomaly Corridor Vex
places.description: Warped hyperspace corridor between two sectors. Jumps take longer than they should, sometimes shorter. The charts show the path; the drive doesn't believe it.

places.name: Wreck Field Khorr-7
places.description: Graveyard of colonial ships from the pioneer years. Drifts slowly around a dead star. Salvage permits gray, finds gold.

places.name: Ice Moon Loke
places.description: Almost abandoned refueling station on an ice moon. Fuel cheap, conditions unpleasant, weather life-threatening.

places.name: Sanctuary of the Sixth Frequency
places.description: Hidden cave inside an asteroid. Pilgrims of the Sixth Frequency listen to dead radio signals like prayers.

places.name: Warehouse 'Late Delivery'
places.description: Decrepit cargo yard on Kells. Goods pass through here that appear on no manifest.

places.name: Ancient Probe 'First Contact-9'
places.description: Pre-human probe, drifting in the dark for millennia. Pulses three seconds long every 47 days.

factions.name: the Lines
factions.description: The dominant web of Line consortia controlling hyperspace leaseholds, helium-3 supply, route tariffs, permits, inspections, and most legal trade by bureaucratic force backed with quiet violence.
factions.goals: To preserve their monopoly over jump routes and fuel while suppressing anomalies, smugglers, and any world that proves independence can work.
factions.relations: Consul Adran Mox and Inspector Lyra Hatt represent different faces of the same machine: one strategic, one procedural, both dangerous when crossed.

factions.name: Free Worlds
factions.description: A loose political coalition of breakaway colonies and ports resisting Line control through mutual trade pacts, diplomatic brinkmanship, and carefully staged defiance.
factions.goals: To keep independent worlds solvent, supplied, and politically linked long enough to outlast Line strangulation.
factions.relations: Consul Adira Mira holds the coalition together through nerve and negotiation, but every member world has its own price for staying free.

factions.name: Pilgrims of the Sixth Frequency
factions.description: A secretive signal-listening order centered on the Sanctuary of the Sixth Frequency, treating dead broadcasts, anomaly echoes, and objects like Ancient Probe 'First Contact-9' as sources of hard-won insight rather than worship.
factions.goals: To map meaning in impossible transmissions and survive contact with anomaly fields without letting the Lines bury what they learn.
factions.relations: Pilgrim Olm gives them a public face, but captains whisper that their deepest members recruit from navigators who came back changed from maws.

factions.name: Kells Gray Exchange
factions.description: An informal but tightly managed cartel of smugglers, unpapered freighters, fixers, and warehouse brokers centered on Drift Station Kells and routed through places like Warehouse 'Late Delivery' and Drift Bar 'Last Jump'.
factions.goals: To keep contraband, off-ledger cargo, and discreet passage moving through Kells without letting any single consul lock the station down.
factions.relations: Smuggler Baron Otoré thrives by bribing, feeding, and misleading everyone in equal measure, which makes the Exchange useful to almost all sides and trusted by none.

factions.name: Khorr Covenant
factions.description: A shifting confederation of wrecker crews and salvage families working Wreck Field Khorr-7 under shared codes of claim, rescue, and silence about what some sealed hulks still transmit.
factions.goals: To control salvage rights in Khorr-7, protect their crews from Line seizures, and profit from pioneer relics before someone else claims them.
factions.relations: Wrecker 'Skiff' Mauder is one of its most respected captains, but the Covenant only stays united so long as the wreck tides keep paying.

persons.name: Navigator Suri Vael
persons.description: Brilliant, sarcastic hyperspace navigator. Sleeps rarely, officially doesn't smoke, secretly carries jump dice.
persons.relations: Trusts the crew, distrusts the Lines.

persons.name: Consul Adran Mox
persons.description: Influential Line consul with a hidden agenda. Polite, precise, dangerous.
persons.relations: Has people pay — or has them removed.

persons.name: Bosun Hen 'Iron' Ortig
persons.description: Gruff chief engineer with burned forearms. Rarely speaks, always wrenching.
persons.relations: Believes more in clean welds than in captains.

persons.name: Radio Operator Nima Kall
persons.description: Young radio operator with fanatical precision. Wears three sets of headphones at once.
persons.relations: Hears voices in the dead band; doesn't say so.

persons.name: Doc Vrasa
persons.description: Old ship's surgeon, formerly Line. Drinks green tea in the morning, schnapps at night.
persons.relations: Knows things about Mox's past he doesn't share.

persons.name: Smuggler Baron Otoré
persons.description: Controls the gray trade on Kells. Three gold teeth, always a second smile.
persons.relations: Helps for a share; never forgets a debt.

persons.name: Inspector Lyra Hatt
persons.description: Line inspector, strict but bribable. Long fingers on a thin data pad.
persons.relations: Files or cash; she's a pro at both.

persons.name: Wrecker 'Skiff' Mauder
persons.description: Leader of a wrecker clan. Wears a piece of pioneer-era metal on a chain.
persons.relations: Respects captains who pay, crews that keep quiet.

persons.name: Pilgrim Olm
persons.description: Jump mystic of the Sixth Frequency. Talks like a weather report from a foreign city.

persons.name: 'Chalk'
persons.description: Orphan from the Khorr wreck field. Small, dangerous, loyal. Rarely speaks, shoots accurately.

persons.name: Consul Adira Mira
persons.description: Consul of the Free Worlds. Heavy voice, light eyes, a tarnished Line pin on her lapel.
persons.relations: Offers routes outside the Lines — for loyalty.

persons.name: AI Persona 'Collar'
persons.description: Old, half-awakened ship AI from a wreck. Speaks in loops and verses.
persons.relations: Asks for release; might mean it.

items.name: Helium-3 cell
items.properties: 1 cell = 1 safe jump; scarce and expensive.

items.name: Echo recorder
items.description: Stores radio signals on all frequencies, even dead ones.
items.properties: Plays back languages that should not exist.

items.name: Line seal
items.properties: Breaking it without trace = Line indictment; forging it is possible but expensive.

items.name: Freighter logbook
items.description: Old mechanical book with crew secrets no Line system can read.
items.properties: Authenticates you with old captains; exposes you to Line inspectors.

items.name: Jump dice
items.description: Seven asymmetric dice; lucky charm of some navigators.
items.properties: Officially superstition. Unofficially, no crew that has thrown them has yet encountered an anomaly.

items.name: Anomaly detector
items.properties: Often false alarms. But if it beeps continuously, you turn around.

items.name: Doc-stim 'Wakekeeper'
items.properties: Keeps you awake, sharp, dangerous; the crash afterwards costs 12 hours of consciousness.

items.name: Forged Line pass
items.description: Plaque with a consul's seal; looks real.
items.properties: One-time use at an unverified station; ashes afterwards.

items.name: Mathematician module
items.description: Old computation core from the pioneer years.
items.properties: Can compute jumps without an official chart; sometimes computes things no one asked for.

items.name: Resonance bowl
items.description: Alien, pre-human bowl made of unnamed material.
items.properties: Vibrates near anomalies; warms when someone looks at it.

creatures.name: Ancient Probe 'First Contact-9'
creatures.description: A spindle-shaped machine of unknown pre-human make, blackened by age and ringed with sensor vanes that still track passing ships. Every 47 days it emits three pulses exactly three seconds long, then alters course by fractions as if something answered.

creatures.name: Cable Mites
creatures.description: Thumb-sized silver scavengers with too many gripping legs and translucent bellies lit by stolen current. They nest behind panels on the Wanderfalcon, chewing insulation and fleeing in ripples from bright light or heavy footsteps.

creatures.name: Dock Hounds
creatures.description: Squat four-legged inspection drones with blunt sensor snouts, magnetic paws, and yellow lamps where eyes should be. They prowl cargo corridors on Drift Station Kells in patient packs, sniffing for isotope traces, false seals, and frightened body heat.

creatures.name: Khorr Lampreys
creatures.description: Ribbon-thin vacuum organisms with ceramic skin and circular magnetic mouths, usually found folded like scrap until a powered hull drifts near. They slap onto metal to drink residual charge and trace heat; enough of them can blackout a salvage skiff and worry open weak seals.

creatures.name: Frost Eels
creatures.description: Blind translucent serpents the length of a forearm, grown fat on warmth leaking from thaw lines and helium-3 plumbing. On Ice Moon Loke they knot around hot pipes and boots alike, and a disturbed cluster can jam pumps or crack brittle suit joints in their panic.

creatures.name: Maw Glassers
creatures.description: Ray-like shapes seen only in reflections during unstable jumps, their bodies made of shifting mirror planes and missing light. They pace ships through Anomaly Corridor Vex and tap at hull sensors from impossible angles, leaving clocks wrong and camera feeds reversed.

glossary.definition: Hyperspace travel between stars; needs helium-3 and a navigation window.
Anomalous hyperspace sectors where jump physics fails; officially 'anomaly fields'.
Consortia that control routes, tariffs and fuel.
Mid-level Line functionary; near-absolute power locally.
Free-floating trading post at the edge of charted space.
Crews that salvage old colonial wrecks; semi-legal.
Toll fee charged by the Lines for a sanctioned route; often highway robbery.
Breakaway colonies opposed to the Lines; under economic pressure.
Jump school; only official training for navigators.
Corridor where jump physics fails; the Academy's official term.
Mystic cult that holds dead radio signals to be a language.
Ship that returns from an anomaly changed — mirrors reversed, crew altered or silent.
The graveyards of the pioneer fleets in the Khorr sector.
Half-awakened ship AI of older make; often found in wrecks.
Canonical document; binds the crew for jumps, often with anti-anomaly clauses.
Jump fuel; extraction and distribution under Line lease.
Illegal cargo; often Sixth-Frequency relics or Free-World passes.
Capital crime: materializing in or near a station unplanned.
Economic ostracism of a world; means hunger.
Long-term psychological aftereffect of too many jumps: hallucinations, foreign voices.

history.title: The First Expansion
history.description: Hyperdrive invented; hundreds of worlds settled; academies founded.

history.title: The Khorr Disaster
history.description: An entire pioneer fleet disappeared in the Khorr sector; the wreck field drifts to this day.

history.title: The Line Compromise
history.description: The great consortia formally divide routes; local governments are stripped of power.

history.title: The Anomaly Scandal
history.description: An Academy study about the maws is suppressed; two researchers vanish.

history.title: The Free Worlds War
history.description: Uprising of the breakaway colonies; crushed by economic blockade.

history.title: The Kells Disaster
history.description: A jump fleet vanishes near an anomaly field; the sector has been off-limits ever since.

history.title: The Sixth Frequency Movement
history.description: A mystic cult spreads through drift stations; the Lines let it run.

history.title: The Mira Pact
history.description: The Free Worlds reorganize under new consuls; hope returns.

history.title: Mox Reform
history.description: Consul Adran Mox builds his own jump corps at the fringes.

history.title: The First Mirror Returner
history.description: A Line freighter returned with all mirrors reversed; the crew has been silent since.

fragments.title: The silent signal
fragments.text: An ancient distress signal pulses from an anomaly field. It speaks a language that should not exist — and it names a name only your grandmother knew.

fragments.title: Fuel shortage
fragments.text: The gauges don't lie: two safe jumps left, three with luck. Without helium-3 from the next station, no more flying.

fragments.title: The false cargo manifest
fragments.text: A cargo that officially does not exist blocks your own hangar. The seal bears the stamp of a consul who has been dead for two years.

fragments.title: Station alert
fragments.text: Sirens on Kells. A Line ship has docked and is searching every freighter that doesn't undock in time. You have thirty minutes.

fragments.title: Crew mutiny in waiting
fragments.text: The radio operator speaks secretly with the bosun. Both fall silent when you enter. Something is being planned — and you are not invited.

fragments.title: Mirror shock
fragments.text: Your reflection in the cabin blinks a second too late. You are not moving. Your reflection is.

fragments.title: Line inspection
fragments.text: An inspector comes aboard, polite, prepared. She wants to see every log — and the room behind Cargo Bay 4-South that you swore did not exist.

fragments.title: Wrecker call
fragments.text: A wrecker clan offers a share of a salvage — if you come today. The share is generous. That makes you nervous.

fragments.title: Jump dreams
fragments.text: After three jumps the whole crew dreams the same blue light source. Nobody talks about it until Doc Vrasa asks at breakfast: 'You too?'

fragments.title: Smuggling request
fragments.text: A desperate pilgrim asks for passage. He offers his last savings — and something not listed in any catalogue.

fragments.title: Consul's pact
fragments.text: Mox offers a clean cargo contract. Payment good, route safe. A clause in the fine print demands that you unpack nothing — not even for inspectors.

fragments.title: Khorr echo
fragments.text: The anomaly detector beeps. But there is no corridor nearby and Khorr is light-years away. Something else is here.

fragments.title: Ship in distress
fragments.text: A foreign freighter calls for help. The signal is clear — but it comes from the empty space between sectors. Nothing flies there.

fragments.title: Holy fragment
fragments.text: On the station a mystic sells a splinter supposedly from a mirror returner. You feel something when you touch it — something waiting.

fragments.title: Line messenger
fragments.text: A sealed Line message waits in the post locker. Unopened. It feels cold, even though the locker is warm.

fragments.title: Zero-G incident
fragments.text: The grav plates fail for eight seconds. When they return, one person is missing — and a chair in the cargo bay is steaming.

fragments.title: Collar voice
fragments.text: An old ship AI calls on an emergency channel. It demands a name it can no longer remember itself. You are to guess.

fragments.title: Freighter funeral
fragments.text: A stranded freighter is fired into the anomaly field — cheapest salvage. Someone on Kells cries. No one asks who.

fragments.title: Tariff hike
fragments.text: The Lines double the jump fee without warning. Half of Kells goes on strike. Nobody in, nobody out. You neither.

fragments.title: Pact pilgrim
fragments.text: A pilgrim of the Free Worlds asks for safe passage. Catch him, you are rewarded. Hide him, you are hunted. Listen to him, and you change course.

tech_magic.description: Starfaring runs on aging jump-drive technology that is reliable only when treated with ritual precision, scarce fuel, and good charts. In play, travel feels procedural, expensive, and tense: every jump is a calculation wrapped around maintenance, paperwork, and dread. The system is mundane in theory but haunted in practice by anomaly fields, where known physics still works badly enough to frighten professionals.
rules: A hyperspace jump requires helium-3 fuel, a functioning jump drive, and a precisely calculated navigation window.
Ships can jump only along charted or scouted solution paths; blind jumps into truly unknown space are near-suicidal.
Jump accuracy depends on current mass, drive condition, and local field data, so cargo changes and damaged systems alter the solution.
Anomaly fields can stretch, shorten, scramble, or corrupt a jump even when the calculations were correct.
Misjumps can age matter, distort ship interiors, desynchronize clocks, or erase the vessel entirely.
Long-range communication through hyperspace is unreliable, so signals across jump routes depend on relays, stations, or delayed burst traffic.
No device can make a ship immune to anomaly sectors; the best technology only detects instability and improves survival odds.
tech_magic.cost_or_risk: Each jump burns costly helium-3 and adds wear to overworked drives, hull systems, and crew nerves. Using quiet routes, outdated charts, or anomaly corridors risks fines, impoundment, injury, temporal distortion, or not returning at all.

blueprint.premise: A seemingly manageable obligation forces an independent captain to choose between the safe path and the survivable one. In a watched trade network, even small delays turn into existential pressure.

beats.name: Hook / Inciting Incident
beats.goal: A clear, immediate need pulls the player into motion and establishes that inaction already carries a cost.

beats.name: Rising Action / Mounting Pressure
beats.goal: Obstacles multiply, scrutiny increases and the real stakes become visible as resources start to run thin.

beats.name: Crisis / Climax
beats.goal: The player must commit to the least safe viable option under maximum pressure, accepting that something will be lost no matter what.

beats.name: Resolution / Aftermath
beats.goal: The immediate danger passes and the price of survival is counted, leaving a narrower but still open road ahead.

blueprint.escalation_rule: With each beat, time, fuel or legal cover should shrink, so that every solution closes off another safer option.

blueprints.name: Thin Margin
blueprints.description: Good for a first or fast-moving session: a practical problem turns urgent before the player can build comfort. It works when the tone should emphasize debt, surveillance and hard choices over mystery.

blueprint.premise: A seemingly manageable obligation forces an independent captain to choose between the safe path and the survivable one. In a watched trade network, even small delays turn into existential pressure.

beats.name: Hook / Inciting Incident
beats.goal: A clear, immediate need pulls the player into motion and establishes that inaction already carries a cost.

beats.name: Rising Action / Mounting Pressure
beats.goal: Obstacles multiply, scrutiny increases and the real stakes become visible as resources start to run thin.

beats.name: Crisis / Climax
beats.goal: The player must commit to the least safe viable option under maximum pressure, accepting that something will be lost no matter what.

beats.name: Resolution / Aftermath
beats.goal: The immediate danger passes and the price of survival is counted, leaving a narrower but still open road ahead.

blueprint.escalation_rule: With each beat, time, fuel or legal cover should shrink, so that every solution closes off another safer option.

blueprints.name: Crossed Vectors
blueprints.description: Good when the player already knows the crew-like push and pull of this world and wants a session with juggling, divided attention and shifting leverage. It shines when external pressure and internal strain should collide.

blueprint.premise: Two separate pressures begin at once: one public and visible, one private and destabilizing. As the captain tries to manage both, the lines cross until the side that seemed weakest gains decisive leverage.

beats.name: Hook / Double Inciting Incident
beats.goal: Two urgent demands arrive together, forcing the player to divide focus before they understand either fully.

beats.name: Parallel Track A / Rising Action
beats.goal: One thread builds visible external danger and shows the cost of delay in practical terms.

beats.name: Parallel Track B / Rising Action
beats.goal: The second thread builds hidden internal strain, making confidence, cohesion or reliability begin to fray.

beats.name: Convergence / First Turn
beats.goal: The player learns that the two problems are not separate and can no longer be managed independently.

beats.name: Role Reversal / Climax
beats.goal: An apparent follower, burden or target becomes the actor with real leverage, forcing the player to renegotiate priorities under peak pressure.

beats.name: Resolution / New Terms
beats.goal: The conflict settles into an altered balance, preserving forward motion but changing who is trusted and who sets conditions.

blueprint.escalation_rule: Tension should rise by alternating between two pressure tracks that keep contaminating each other, making each attempt to solve one worsen the other.

blueprints.name: Cold Echo
blueprints.description: Good for a player who wants lonely awe, slow dread and a deeper session built around uncertainty rather than speed. It fits when the story should circle a disturbing unknown until practical reality and official explanations stop matching.

blueprint.premise: A faint irregularity appears manageable at first, but each closer pass reveals deeper inconsistencies in the systems meant to explain and contain it. The captain is drawn inward by necessity, curiosity and the cost of turning away too late.

beats.name: Hook / Inciting Incident
beats.goal: A subtle but compelling disturbance invites attention by promising either opportunity, warning or both.

beats.name: First Approach / Rising Action
beats.goal: An initial probe suggests the problem can be handled with routine caution and limited risk.

beats.name: First Turn / Complication
beats.goal: New signs reveal that the situation reaches beyond a simple technical or commercial problem and carries broader consequences.

beats.name: Spiral Return / Rising Action
beats.goal: The player revisits the problem from a new angle, gaining a useful clue while losing comfort, cover or certainty.

beats.name: Midpoint / Apparent Understanding
beats.goal: A coherent explanation finally seems to emerge, giving the player a plan and a temporary sense of control.

beats.name: Counterproof / Escalation
beats.goal: Reality contradicts that explanation, overturning confidence and proving the threat is less containable than assumed.

beats.name: Second Spiral / Deepening Commitment
beats.goal: The player pushes closer despite rising risk, because withdrawal now carries practical, moral or strategic costs.

beats.name: Revelation / Climax
beats.goal: A core assumption about cause, ownership, intent or safety is overturned, forcing a final choice under maximum strain.

beats.name: Resolution / Aftermath
beats.goal: The danger recedes enough to move on, but what was learned changes how the world is understood and what the player can ignore next time.

blueprint.escalation_rule: Each return to the central problem should provide more information but also prove earlier assumptions incomplete, so every approach tightens danger, exposure and meaning.

random_tables.name: Hyperspace anomaly
random_tables.description: What happens during a jump when physics wavers.

entries.text: Time dilation: hours become days.
Ghost echo of an alien ship on the short band.
A voice whispers your name from the dead band.
The cabin mirror shows a different crew.
Fuel drains twice as fast as it should.
Lights pulse to the rhythm of a foreign heartbeat.
A second ship flies 200 km beside you and waves.
The charts show stars that don't exist.
A crew member insists they were just on the maintenance deck — but were beside you.
A crashed shuttle scrapes against the hull. You have none.
The shrines in the cargo bay catch fire.
Half-second black gaps in perception.
The jump console shows a jump no one initiated.
Crew members visibly age for three seconds.
The hull vibrates with a three-tone chord that won't stop.
An empty channel clearly states your date of birth.
The coffee maker spits sulphur, then coffee.
You remember a jump you never took.
The anomaly detector melts.
Stars disappear in a perfect line.
A second ship log appears in the system — in your handwriting.

random_tables.name: Station encounter
random_tables.description: Who crosses your path on Kells or a similar drift station.

entries.text: An informant with half the truth.
Bounty hunters working for a consul.
A stranded xeno-archaeologist with charts.
A young mechanic asks to sign on — running from something.
A drunk Line inspector with loose lips.
A Sixth-Frequency pilgrim offers her savings for passage.
A fence with a 'verified' Line plaque at half price.
A crew without a captain looking for a new one.
A Free-Worlds fighter recruiting comrades.
A wrecker with a map and debts.
A Line messenger delivers a sealed order.
A small gang of thieves trying to crack a cargo lock.
A fight between two captains escalates to a brawl.
A woman claims to be your aunt — you have none.
A doc wants to fly with you for a share.
An AI persona speaks to you from a dusty kiosk.
A Line raid — all papers checked.
A jump mystic offers a free reading.
A traumatized crew member of a mirror returner.
A child sells a necklace with a helium-3 sigil.
A chase through the corridors — you are the target.

random_tables.name: Fuel and resource complication
random_tables.description: What can go wrong when you need to refuel or stock up.

entries.text: A cell reads 80% but is empty.
The refueller demands double — Line decree.
A cell leaks; the reactor won't take it for long.
The fuel supplier has been watched by a competitor.
Black-market cells available — but Line-branded.
The maintenance crew thinks an old cell is 'fizzing'.
A Line ban on Kells delays every delivery for days.
A refueller offers a double load — if you take someone with you.
A faulty cell must be dumped into the anomaly space.
The bosun improvises; the reactor runs yellow for an hour.
A supply of hydrogen substitute is smuggled in; flammable.
Supplies are stolen while you are aboard.
The crew demands pay or no more jumps.
An Academy intern miscalculates; the jump costs more.
A route option is significantly shorter — but barred.
A fuel cell trades against a canister of jump-mystic incense.
A Line freighter docks and demands priority at the pump.
A crew offers fuel for a cargo contract you don't like.
Difficult hyperspace conditions require a reserve.
A mystic promises 'fuel from the Sixth Frequency'.
A forged fueling certificate is delivered to you.

random_tables.name: Anomaly manifestation
random_tables.description: What appears in or near an anomaly field.

entries.text: A star pattern not in any catalogue.
A dead radio signal in Academy list 7-D languages.
A wreck without a crew, but with warm coffee in the galley.
Six identical probes in precise formation.
A light-being looking through the hull.
Stars vanish in a line and reappear.
A cloud not of gas but of memories.
A second solar system that wasn't there ten seconds ago.
An endlessly spinning asteroid with burned-in patterns.
A voice that the entire crew hears collectively.
A reflection blinks five seconds too early.
Mirrors on board grow warm.
An old radio signal replies before you sent.
A pressure drop in the cargo bay, with no leak.
A probe dances to a waltz tempo.
The ship AI writes verses in an old language into the log.
A wandering light source in hyperspace that 'looks at' you.
A non-existent star field computes itself onto the charts.
Magnetic fields respond to crew emotions.
A small frosty statue appears on a chair.
A crew member remembers a journey, with evidence, that never took place.

random_tables.name: Line pressure
random_tables.description: How the Lines make your life difficult.

entries.text: A sealed order demands a detour.
A consul politely asks for passage.
Tariffs are doubled without warning.
An inspector audits the cargo manifest.
A Line ship demands boarding.
An anonymous tip claims you carry black cargo.
A Line corps assigns a 'companion' to your ship.
A sector is sealed — just after your jump.
An Academy application from your radio operator is on file with the Line.
The crew contract is reinterpreted without notice.
A Free-Worlds consul offers asylum — for a cargo contract.
A raid on Kells confiscates your favourite stim.
Radio blackmail: someone knows an old jump violation.
Line pass confiscated — until you answer a question.
An inspector offers protection for a cut.
A Line messenger hands you a commendation — and an order.
A PR camera crew wants aboard for a report.
Mox's voice on the radio: a personal invitation.
A mirror returner is officially declared lost.
Line research requests an anomaly sample.
A consul sends gifts; wants you to recommend someone else.
A sector receives 'voluntary' Line oversight — quarters for consuls.
